story.description: A clip of conservation, restoration, habitats, reforestation, or protected nature: scuba divers restoring coral reefs in lombok
story.hook: Mangroves can slow waves before they hit land.
story.lead: Mangroves can slow waves before they hit land. Watch the roots in the water, because mangroves create a rough barrier that breaks up wave energy. They also shelter young fish and hold muddy coastlines together. Restoration can be infrastructure made of roots. Which repair story next?
story.script: Mangroves can slow waves before they hit land. Watch the roots in the water, because mangroves create a rough barrier that breaks up wave energy. They also shelter young fish and hold muddy coastlines together. Restoration can be infrastructure made of roots. Which repair story next?

story.description: A clip of Earth, atmosphere, clouds, storms, or landscapes seen from above: white clouds seen from an airplane window
story.hook: Storm shows why the cloud spiral matters.
story.lead: Those clouds aren't solid, they're floating water. Look closer and you see sharp edges stop mid-air. That's why pilots ride above them instead of through—thin air can't hold all that weight. So next time you're up there, wave at the ghostly layer beneath you.
story.script: Those clouds aren't solid, they're floating water. Look closer and you see sharp edges stop mid-air. That's why pilots ride above them instead of through—thin air can't hold all that weight. So next time you're up there, wave at the ghostly layer beneath you.

story.description: A clip of dogs / puppies: a woman teaching dogs at home
story.hook: Dogs build a map with smell
story.lead: They read your breath in a second. Watch their nostrils flare—that’s the moment they lock in your scent. Dogs process breath molecules faster than we blink because their nose has a direct shortcut to the brain. That’s why they greet you soft when you’re calm, tense when you’re not. Ever notice how they hesitate before you even speak?
story.script: They read your breath in a second. Watch their nostrils flare—that’s the moment they lock in your scent. Dogs process breath molecules faster than we blink because their nose has a direct shortcut to the brain. That’s why they greet you soft when you’re calm, tense when you’re not. Ever notice how they hesitate before you even speak?
